author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
It Does Not Detract from God’s Almighty Power, that He is Incapable of Either Sinning, or Dying, or Destroying Himself.
title: It Does Not Detract from God’s Almighty Power, that He is Incapable of Either Sinning, or Dying, or Destroying Himself.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Chapter 57 [XLIX.]—It Does Not Detract from God’s Almighty Power, that He is Incapable of Either Sinning, or Dying, or Destroying Himself.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: “Inasmuch,” says he, “as not to sin is ours, we are able to sin and to avoid sin.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: What, then, if another should say: “Inasmuch as not to wish for unhappiness is ours, we are able both to wish for it and not to wish for it?”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: And yet we are positively unable to wish for it.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: For who could possibly wish to be unhappy, even though he wishes for something else from which unhappiness will ensue to him against his will?

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Then again, inasmuch as, in an infinitely greater degree, it is God’s not to sin, shall we therefore venture to say that He is able both to sin and to avoid sin?

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: God forbid that we should ever say that He is able to sin!

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: For He cannot, as foolish persons suppose, therefore fail to be almighty, because He is unable to die, or because He cannot deny Himself.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: What, therefore, does he mean? by what method of speech does he try to persuade us on a point which he is himself loth to consider?

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: For he advances a step further, and says: “Inasmuch as, however, it is not of us to be able to avoid sin; even if we were to wish not to be able to avoid sin, it is not in our power to be unable to avoid sin.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: It is an involved sentence, and therefore a very obscure one.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: It might, however, be more plainly expressed in some such way as this: “Inasmuch as to be able to avoid sin is not of us, then, whether we wish it or do not wish it, we are able to avoid sin!”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: He does not say, “Whether we wish it or do not wish it, we do not sin,”—for we undoubtedly do sin, if we wish;—but yet he asserts that, whether we will or not, we have the capacity of not sinning,—a capacity which he declares to be inherent in our nature.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Of a man, indeed, who has his legs strong and sound, it may be said admissibly enough, “whether he will or not he has the capacity of walking;” but if his legs be broken, however much he may wish, he has not the capacity.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: The nature of which our author speaks is corrupted.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: “Why is dust and ashes proud?”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: It is corrupted.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: It implores the Physician’s help.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: “Save me, O Lord,” is its cry; “Heal my soul,” it exclaims.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Why does he check such cries so as to hinder future health, by insisting, as it were, on its present capacity?
